title: See Inside Typhoon Mangkhut in 3-D - The New York Times
headline: See Inside Typhoon Mangkhut in 3-D

beats.text: See Inside Typhoon Mangkhut in 3-D
By YULIYA PARSHINA-KOTTAS, KARTHIK PATANJALI, JEREMY WHITE and BENJAMIN WILHELM SEPT. 15, 2018
A NASA satellite captured the intense rainfall of 2018’s strongest storm so far.
Before it made landfall in the Philippines on Saturday, Typhoon Mangkhut was the most powerful storm so far of 2018, with heavy rains and winds that reached up to 170 miles per hour.
It was also the strongest storm to hit the region since 2013, when Typhoon Haiyan killed more than 6,000 people.
Initial death counts were far lower than Philippine officials had feared in the days before the storm made landfall. But it will likely take days or weeks before the true toll is known.
The typhoon lost strength after it passed over the Philippines and headed toward southern China, and it was expected to weaken further as it made landfall there on Sunday.
For the latest updates on Typhoon Mangkhut, see our live briefing and tracking map. If you want to help those affected by the typhoon, here’s how.
Additional production by Jon Huang.
Sources: NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, NASA Global Precipitation Measurement, NASA Scientific Visualization Studio.
